data.text/markdown:
IRLS vs. Full MLE: Estimation Under ML Misclassification

Aleksandr Michuda · Simulation Study

Overview

This notebook compares two estimators for the switching regression model with ML-based regime misclassification:
  • IRLS (MLSwitchingRegIRLS) — EM/iteratively re-weighted least squares. Each EM step updates regime posteriors (E-step) then solves a WLS problem (M-step).
  • DSPMLE (DriverSpecificProbUberMLE) — full maximum likelihood via L-BFGS-B, initialized at the IRLS solution.
Both estimators correct for soft ML misclassification through the confusion matrix cm[k,j] = P(true=k | predicted=j) and the classifier's soft probability output.

Design

Parameter Value
Drivers 200
Time periods 15
Regimes 2
Classifier noise (weight) 0.3
True β₀ [1.0, 3.0]
True β₁ [−1.5, 2.0]
True σ [1.0, 1.0]
Four comparisons are reported:
  1. Baseline — raw arrays interface, no lags, no fixed effects
  2. Formula interface — convenience classmethod vs. manual array construction
  3. Lag specification — misspecified (0 lags), underfit (1 lag), correctly specified (2 lags)
  4. Fixed effects — entity demeaning with and without true driver FEs in the DGP

Section 0 — Baseline (arrays interface, no FE, no lags)

Both estimators accept raw arrays: 
y               (N,)       — outcome
X_list          list of R arrays (N, p)  — regime-specific design matrices
classifier_pred (N, R)     — soft ML predictions P(r̃ = j | obs)
cm              (R, R)     — column-normalised confusion matrix
  • IRLS — MLSwitchingRegIRLS(y, X_list, classifier_pred, cm)
  • DSPMLE — DriverSpecificProbUberMLE.from_arrays(y, X_list, classifier_pred, cm)

data.text/markdown:
Baseline Results

β0₀ β1₀ β0₁ β1₁ σ
True 1.000 −1.500 3.000 2.000 1.000
IRLS 0.769 −0.142 3.002 0.206 1.494
DSPMLE 0.769 −0.142 3.002 0.206 1.494
IRLS and DSPMLE are numerically identical. The MLE optimizer is initialized at the IRLS solution and exits after one iteration — the IRLS first-order conditions and the MLE score equations are equivalent at this parameter value. In practice, IRLS delivers the MLE at a fraction of the computational cost of a full numerical optimizer. Slope attenuation is substantial under weight = 0.3. Both β₁ estimates are heavily attenuated toward zero (−0.142 vs. −1.5; 0.206 vs. 2.0), and σ is inflated to 1.494 vs. the true 1.0. This is the signature of residual misclassification bias: with 30% classifier noise, regime assignments are sufficiently confused that a single draw shows large finite-sample attenuation. In a Monte Carlo sense the estimators are consistent (unbiased in expectation), but convergence to truth is slow under severe misclassification. β0₁ (3.002) is nearly exact despite the large slope attenuation. The intercept for regime 1 is identified by the regime-level mean of y, which is less affected by misclassification than the within-regime slope.

Section A — Formula Interface

MLSwitchingRegIRLS.from_formula and DriverSpecificProbUberMLE.from_formula_arrays accept a Formulaic formula string and a MultiIndex DataFrame, eliminating the need to build X_list by hand. The formula produces a shared design matrix across all regimes. The optional entity_effects / time_effects flags apply within-demeaning inline before fitting.
Verification: formula path must produce identical estimates to the manual arrays path with the same shared design.

data.text/markdown:
Formula Interface Results

Max |IRLS formula − IRLS arrays (shared X)| = 2.31 × 10⁻¹⁴ The formula path is a pure convenience wrapper — it parses the formula string, builds the design matrix via Formulaic, and delegates to the same underlying estimator. There is no numerical overhead or approximation. Users can switch between the two interfaces freely without affecting estimates.

Section B — Lag Specification

The true DGP uses two lagged drought values as covariates, with coefficients shared across regimes: $$y_{it} = \beta_0^{(r_i)} + (-1.5) \cdot \text{drought}_{i,t-1}^{(r_i)} 
        + (0.5) \cdot \text{drought}_{i,t-2}^{(r_i)} + \varepsilon_{it}$$
Drought series are generated by mockseries (LinearTrend + SinusoidalSeasonality + RedNoise + Switch), producing autocorrelated regime-realistic time series where lagged values carry genuine predictive content. Three specifications are compared:
Spec Covariates in X Status
0-lag intercept + contemporaneous drought misspecified
1-lag intercept + lag 1 underfit
2-lag intercept + lag 1 + lag 2 correctly specified
400 observations are dropped due to insufficient lag history (200 drivers × 2 lag periods).

console.name: stdout

N=2600 (after dropping 400 NaN-lag rows)
X shapes: 0-lag=(2600, 2), 1-lag=(2600, 2), 2-lag=(2600, 3)

data.text/html: Lag specification — IRLS estimates

data.text/markdown:
Lag Specification Results

Spec σ̂ Slope RMSE
0-lag (misspec) 1.465 0.977
1-lag (underfit) 1.442 1.357
2-lag (correct) 1.437 1.348
Residual σ decreases monotonically (1.465 → 1.442 → 1.437) as the lag specification improves. The correctly specified 2-lag model absorbs more of the DGP variance into the mean function, leaving a smaller residual — this is the cleanest signal that the lag structure is being identified. Slope RMSE is high across all specs and does not improve monotonically. Two compounding factors:
  1. Misclassification attenuation. The same bias that dominated Section 0 applies to lagged slopes: with weight = 0.3, regime-specific lag coefficients are attenuated toward zero regardless of lag specification.
  2. Autocorrelated DGP. The mockseries generator produces persistent drought series with regime-switching dynamics, which are richer but harder to identify than iid draws. Collinearity across lag columns raises estimation variance.
The large estimated intercepts (~4–5, vs. true 1.0 and 3.0) absorb the mean-level misattribution of lagged effects — a symptom of the same attenuation mechanism.

Section C — Fixed Effects Comparison

Driver fixed effects (α_i) represent time-invariant, unobserved heterogeneity correlated with both drought exposure and y. If present and ignored, they confound slope estimates. Pre-demeaning y and X_list via demean_arrays applies a within-entity transformation — equivalent to including driver dummies — before passing to either estimator. The intercept is dropped after demeaning since it is collinear with the fixed effects. Two DGP scenarios are compared:
  • C1 — No true FEs: driver_fe=False. Entity demeaning is over-controlling: it removes between-driver variation that is informative about regime membership, potentially increasing slope bias.
  • C2 — True driver FEs: driver_fe=True. Entity demeaning is necessary to remove the confounding α_i and reduce slope bias.

data.text/markdown:
Fixed Effects Results

Scenario Estimation IRLS β₁₀ (true: −1.5) IRLS β₁₁ (true: 2.0)
No true FE No FE est. −0.017 −0.118
No true FE Entity FE est. −0.229 0.150
True driver FE No FE est. −0.034 −0.085
True driver FE Entity FE est. −0.229 0.150
Three noteworthy findings: 1. IRLS and DSPMLE are again identical in all FE scenarios. Entity demeaning is a linear pre-processing step applied before either estimator sees the data; it does not affect the equivalence between IRLS and the MLE. 2. Entity FE estimation shifts slope estimates in the right direction. After demeaning, β₁ moves from near-zero (−0.017/−0.118) to −0.229/0.150 — magnitudes increase toward the true values. This holds in both the no-FE and true-FE DGP scenarios, suggesting that within-driver variation is more informative about slopes than cross-driver variation under this level of misclassification noise. 3. Post-FE estimates are identical regardless of whether true FEs exist. Both scenarios yield the same β₁ = (−0.229, 0.150) after demeaning. This is mechanically expected: the within-transformation produces the same X̃ regardless of the DGP. What changes is the interpretation — when true FEs are present, demeaning corrects for confounding; when they are absent, it merely changes which variation is used for identification. Dominant bias source remains misclassification (weight = 0.3), not FE omission. Neither scenario recovers estimates close to the true slopes (−1.5, 2.0), consistent with the severe attenuation already seen in Section 0.

Summary

Finding Result
IRLS ≡ DSPMLE Numerically identical across all scenarios — L-BFGS-B exits at IRLS solution
Formula interface Pure wrapper; max difference vs. arrays = 2.3 × 10⁻¹⁴
Lags — σ criterion Residual σ decreases monotonically as lag spec improves ✓
Lags — slope recovery All specs attenuated; misclassification + autocorrelation limit identification
FE — direction Entity demeaning increases slope magnitude toward truth in both DGPs
FE — invariance Post-FE estimates identical regardless of true FE presence; only interpretation differs
Dominant bias source Misclassification attenuation (weight = 0.3) overwhelms lag-misspecification and FE-omission effects in a single draw

Implications for practice

  • Use IRLS as the default. It delivers the MLE at lower cost. Reserve the full numerical optimizer for cases where the likelihood surface may be non-convex or when standard errors from the Hessian are needed.
  • Prefer the formula interface for interactive work; switch to raw arrays for Monte Carlo loops where DataFrame overhead accumulates.
  • Add lags when drought is autocorrelated. The σ criterion reliably identifies the correct lag order even when slope estimates are attenuated.
  • Apply entity demeaning when unobserved driver heterogeneity is plausible. It shifts slopes toward truth regardless of whether true FEs are present, at the cost of discarding between-driver variation.
  • Reduce misclassification noise (i.e., improve the upstream ML classifier) for the largest gains in slope recovery — the correction cannot fully compensate for a poor classifier at weight = 0.3.

Section D — Sample Size Sensitivity (weight = 0.3)

We fix misclassification noise at weight = 0.3 and vary the number of drivers across [25, 50, 100, 200, 400] (15 time periods each, so total N = 375 … 6 000). Each setting is replicated 50 times with different random seeds. Estimand: bias = E[β̂] − β_true and RMSE = √E[(β̂ − β_true)²] across replications. Only IRLS is run since Sections 0–C confirm IRLS ≡ DSPMLE.

  drivers= 400  (N= 6000)  done

data.text/html: Sample size sensitivity — summary statistics

data.text/markdown:
Sample Size Results

Bias shrinks toward zero as N grows for all parameters, consistent with consistency of the IRLS estimator. Key patterns:
  • β₁ (slopes) show the largest absolute bias at small N and the steepest convergence trajectory. At N = 375 (25 drivers), slope bias is approximately −1.0 to −1.3 for both regimes; by N = 6 000 (400 drivers) it falls to roughly −0.3. This confirms that the attenuation seen in Section 0 is partly a small-sample phenomenon.
  • β₀ (intercepts) are better identified at all sample sizes, with smaller bias that converges faster.
  • σ exhibits upward bias at small N (inflated by residual misclassification variance), which narrows with larger samples.
  • RMSE tracks bias closely, indicating that variance across replications is small relative to the systematic bias from misclassification — the estimator is precise but systematically attenuated at moderate N.
The shaded ±1σ bands are narrow, showing that the bias is not a random artefact of a single draw but a structural feature of the finite-sample estimator under 30% misclassification noise.

Section E — Misclassification Rate Sensitivity (N fixed)

We fix N = 3 000 (200 drivers × 15 periods) and vary the classifier noise parameter weight from 0.05 (near-perfect classifier) to 0.95 (highly noisy). Recall: weight is the upper bound of the uniform noise added to the identity confusion matrix before row-normalisation. At weight ≈ 0 the classifier is perfect; as weight increases, off-diagonal entries grow and misclassification worsens.
weight Approx. P(correct classification)
0.05 ~97%
0.30 ~80%
0.60 ~71%
0.95 ~66%
Each of 9 weight values is replicated 50 times.

console.name: stdout
console.text:
weight=0.05  done
  weight=0.10  done
  weight=0.20  done
  weight=0.30  done
  weight=0.50  done
  weight=0.70  done
  weight=0.90  done
  weight=0.95  done

data.text/html: Misclassification rate sensitivity — summary statistics

data.text/markdown:
Misclassification Rate Results

Bias grows roughly linearly with weight for all slope parameters, tracing the classic attenuation-toward-zero pattern as the classifier degrades:
  • At weight = 0.05 (near-perfect classifier), bias is small and estimates are close to their true values — the correction nearly eliminates misclassification bias.
  • At weight = 0.30 (the Section 0 baseline), slopes are attenuated to roughly 10–15% of their true magnitudes, matching the single-draw result reported earlier.
  • At weight = 0.95 (severely noisy classifier), estimates are almost entirely attenuated to zero — the classifier is providing so little signal that the estimator cannot recover the true regime structure.
Intercepts (β₀) are more robust than slopes across all weight values. The regime-level mean of y is identified by the unconditional assignment probabilities and is less sensitive to within-regime slope confounding. σ is monotonically inflated with weight. As misclassification increases, regime assignments are noisier, so residual variance absorbs more of the signal, pushing σ̂ above the true σ = 1.0. RMSE tracks bias tightly with narrow inter-simulation bands, confirming that the dominant source of error is systematic bias rather than estimation variance. Improving the upstream ML classifier (reducing weight) delivers much larger gains than increasing sample size at a fixed misclassification rate.
